prompt: Write a 150-word short story about a lighthouse keeper who discovers something unexpected in the fog. Do not use dialogue.
response: Thick fog clung to the cliffs like soaked wool, muting the lighthouse’s pulse. Keeper Rowan climbed the tower’s spiral, footsteps echoing against salt-stained stone. At the lamp room, he trimmed wicks that had burned all night, then wiped condensation from the glass. Through the cloud, a silhouette emerged—tall, angular, motionless—where sea should be. Rowan adjusted the beam slowly; the shape sharpened into a schooner, masts intact, sails furled, yet no crew visible on deck. The vessel drifted against tide and wind, its hull silent. Rowan descended, lantern quivering, and descended the cliff path, boots slipping on wet stone. When he reached the surf, fog thinned; the schooner dissolved into spray, leaving only a single brass compass in tide-foam, needle spinning clockwise, then counterclockwise, then still. Rowan carried it back uphill; the tower light greeted him, steady and certain, yet the compass tugged his palm toward horizons unknown.
